 BREAST CANCER GUIDE : 
WRITTEN FOR THOSE WITHOUT AN ACADEMIC BACKGROUND  IN BIOLOGY  
By 
ARIANNA LEIGH WILLIAMS  
 
____________________  
 
A Thesis Submitted to The Honors College  
In Partial Fulfillment of the Bachelors degree  
With Honors in  
Molecular and Cellular Biology  
THE UNIVERSITY OF ARIZONA  
M A Y  2 0 2 1  
 
 
 
 
Approved by:  
_________________________  
Dr. Andrew Paek  
Department of Molecular and Cellular Biology

3 
 Abstract  
 Throughout my undergraduate career, I have taken many classes that addressed the topic 
of cancer. It is one of the modern mysteries of biology. In discussions with other molecular and 
cellular biology students, one of the most frequent questions that we ar e asked is: “Why haven’t 
they cured cancer yet?” It is a valid question, as well as one that highlights the disconnect that 
seems to exist between current biological research and the general public.  
 My grandmother had breast cancer and it was never fully explained to her what was 
happening within  her body , what the treatments would do to her and the cancer . At least, not in a 
way that she could understand. They used medical  jargon and scientific  names that were 
confusing to her. This inspired me to  write a breast cancer guide for my Honors thesis. The goal 
is to provide accurate scientific information in language that is accessible to someone with or 
without a significant background in biology .  
 “Cancer” is a terrifying word  because its true meaning is unknown to many people. 
Hopefully, this guide can provide valuable information and knowledge that can help cancer seem 
less scary and overwhelming. A greater understanding of the disease could bring comfort to 
cancer patients as  well as their families and friends.

4 
 Basic  Biology  
 To better understand cancer, it helps to have a basic understanding of the human body 
and how it is structured . Whether you are a science -afficionado or haven’t  taken a biology class 
since high school, this section is designed to provide that knowledge.  
 
What is a Cell? 
 A cell is the most basic unit of life.  Every living thing, including you, is comprised of one 
or more cells. There is a great range in the number of cells that create an individual organism. 
The human body is incredibly complex, containing over 30 trillion cells. Baker’s yeast is 
simpler, conta ining only a single cell, but both are living beings.  
 It may help to think of the cells in your body as Legos. They come in many  shapes and 
sizes that together build a greater structure.  In the same way, your body is  an intricate mosaic  of 
different cell types that work in unison to keep you alive. Your nerve cells allow you to feel 
sensations like touch. Your blood cells circulate  to protec t you from infections and provid e 
nutrients throughout your body. Skin cells protect everything inside  from the harsh outer 
environment. Each of these cells has a unique appearance, but all are a part of what makes you 
who you are.  
A collection of similar cell types in one area is called a “tissue.” Some examples of 
tissues include: the skin, lungs, and m uscle s. Each of these tissue types respond to different 
conditions based on the pattern  of cells that compose it. Each  cell types  may look different, but 
they all have similar structures within them. They are all mini compartments that hold all the 
tools n eeded to allow your bodily processes to take place. The differences depend on how they 
use their tools.  
Another key characteristic of cells is that they have the capacity to replicate themselves. 
A cell undergoes a process called “cell division” where it creates a copy of itself. Some cells 
undergo division regularly to repair damage. Skin cells are an excel lent example of cells with

5 
 frequent turno ver. If you scrape your elbow, your skin cells must divide to fill the space left 
behind. Thanks to cell division, your skin eventually returns to normal. Other cells have much 
lower replication rates in adult human s. For example, neurons , the cells that make up the brain,  
stop replicating at a young age. This is why brain damage is so devastating  compared to a skin 
cut or broken bone . The neurons are not able to replenish themselves following trauma. Cell 
replicatio n is a critical process. Cancer can arise from problems in this process, causing the cells 
to replicate without control . An excess production of cells fo rms a tumor . Tumors are not 
inherently cancerous, but they can be the first step .  
 
What is a Gene? 
Most of the life -sustaining work in your cells is performed by molecules called 
“proteins.” There are millions of different kinds of proteins, each with unique functions. The 
instructions for how to make these proteins are encoded in something called “DNA ” which is 
housed in the center of every cell.  
DNA includes the information for how to make everything your body needs. It contains 
smaller units called “genes” that each instruct your cell how to make one protein. You can think 
of DNA as  a recipe book.  Genes represent the  recipe s within the DNA cookboo k. Each gene  
contains the instructions to make a single pr oduct . The final product is a protein that perform s a 
job in the cell.  It is estimated that the human genome contains around 30,000 genes. That’s a 
pretty big cookbook!  
Every cell in your body contains the same DNA  and all  cells have the ability to use any 
gene. However, the fate of a cell is defined by which recipes it make s from the central cookbook. 
A blood cell may need certain proteins that a liver cell does not. They both contain the recipe, 
but a blood cell will prepare the final dish (protein) while the liver cell will not. Different levels 
of each protein within cell  types  is called difference in  “gene expression.” Expression is simply 
the scientific word for production. Cells possess extensive mechanisms to control which  genes 
are expressed , when they are expressed,  and the amount of production that occurs .

6 
 Cancer c an occur when there are errors in the DNA . The genes produce too much, too 
little, or an incorrect version of their protein product.

7 
 What is Cancer?  
 The term “cancer” actually refers to a collection of diseases that have two key 
characteristics: uncontrolled cell growth and invasive capabilities . Cancer occurs when 
something goes wrong in the regulation of normal cellular processes. Cancer cells  replicate too 
quickly  compared to normal cells . This causes a build -up of  cells in a single area, called a 
“tumor.” Eventually , this tumor can become cancerous if it gains the ability to invade other body 
tissues. This process of invasion is called “metastasis”  (muh -tas-tuh-sis).  
Cancers can arise in virtually any cell or tiss ue in the body. Since cancer arises from cells 
within a person’s body, that cancer is as unique as the individual. Even cancers that are in the 
same tissue can look and behave differently between individuals. This can make it difficult to 
prescribe general treatments for cancer. Modern cancer treatment is moving towards 
personalized medicine to help deal with the indiv idualistic nature of cancer.  
 Cancer is considered a disease of self. It arises in a single individual and is not contagious 
to those around you. Since it arises from your own cells, it takes advantage of the systems that 
your body already has in place. T his makes it difficult to target cancer cells for treatments as they 
masquerade as normal cells. The goal of treatment is to avoid harming the body’s healthy tissues 
while killing the cancer cells.  
   
 
What is a Tumor?  
 A “tumor” is an abnormal accumulati on of cells that occurs when cells are replicating too 
quickly. There is nowhere for the new cells to go, thus causing  a build -up of cells in a single 
area. Tumors can occur anywhere in the body  and can vary in size from a tiny growth to a large 
mass. Tumors alone are not always cancerous, but they should always be examined by a medical 
professional.  
 A “benign”  (bih-nahyn)  
tumor is a noncancerous growth. 
This type of tumor lacks invasive 
capabilit ies, meaning it cannot 
enter other body tissues.  They 
also tend to be slow growing. If 
properly removed, a benign 
tumor usually will not reappear.  
 A “malignant” (muh -lig-
nuhnt) tumor is a cancerous 
growth. This type of tumor can 
grow quickly and spread t o other 
parts of the body through a 
process called metastasis . 
Malignant tumors will likely

8 
 require more extensive treatment. It is extremely helpful to catch them early before they have a 
chance to spread to other body tissues.  
 Any abnormal lump or grow th should always be examined by a doctor. Even benign 
tumors can occasionally become premalignant. In this state, they are still not considered 
cancerous but need to be monitored closely for any changes.  
 
 
What Causes Cancer?  
 The primary cause of cancer is the mutation or misregulation of genes. A “mutation” is 
when any change is made to the original sequence of the DNA. The DNA in a cell functions like 
a cookbook with genes being the recipes for various proteins. Genes contain  a series of different 
bases in a string. “Bases” are the building blocks of DNA. The specific order of these bases can 
be translated by the cell into a series of amino acids, the building blocks of proteins. Gene 
mutations can be deletions (removing an or iginal portion), insertions (adding something new), or 
substitutions (changing the base order ). It is just like removing, adding, or substituting 
ingredients in a recipe. Alterations to genes can have a variety of effects on the cell.  
The mutations that c ause cancer often result in the promotion of cell division and the 
suppression of anti -growth signals. It takes multiple mutations in the same cell to develop cancer. 
Many different factors can cause mutations and will be discussed in the following section s.  
 
Random Mutations  
 Every time a cell divides, it splits itself in two. To ensure that both cells are fully 
functional, the original cell must first make a copy of everything inside it. Even the DNA must 
be copied prior to division. During DNA replication, the cell works to c opy three billion bases 
perfectly in sequence. This would be the equivalent of copying down the world’s longest book 
three hundred times by hand. There are bound to be mistakes when copying something this large. 
The cell has mechanisms in place to help pre vent and fix mistakes when they happen. This 
makes mutations extremely rare in the grand scheme of things, especially mutations that have a 
pronounced effect. However, even a good proofreader misses a single typo here and there. Typos 
in DNA are “mutations .” The mutations are then passed along through every subsequent cell 
division.  
 Random mutations have a variety of effects. Most of the time  a single mutation is small 
and will not have a significant effect on the gene and its protein product. However, in  rare cases 
mutations do have an effect. The se effects can have various impacts. The mutations could 
increase or decrease the production of  the affected protein . They could ruin the genetic 
instructions to such an extent that the protein is nonfunctional. On rarer occasions, mutations can 
give proteins new functions entirely. Any type of mutation can potentially promote cancer. 
Cancer results from the misregulation of the cell  replication process. Thus, any mutation that 
affects the process directly or indirectly could lead to cancer. Checkpoints exist to attempt to

9 
 detect DNA damage and correct it . Thus, 
preventing the damage from continuing 
through cell generations. These 
checkpoints  are especially susceptible to 
cancer -causing mutations.  
 There are two main types of genes 
that lead to cancer when mutated : 
oncogenes and tumor suppressors. 
“Oncogenes ” (ong-kuh-jeens) are genes 
that promote growth in the normal cell. 
These gene s are often upregulated in cancer 
to promote rapid cell division. Mutations 
that cause oncogenes to become overactive 
are often cancer -causing. The other type of 
genes are called  “tumor suppressor ” genes. 
These genes, as the name implies, normally 
function  to repress growth and prevent the cell from replicating when DNA damage has 
occurred. Tumor suppressor genes must be deactivated in order for cancer to occur.  
 Your body has two copies of every gene. Oncogenes must only be mutated in one copy to 
promote cancer. Tumor suppressors require that both copies be inactivated to promote cancer. 
This is known as the “two -hit hypothesis. ” Mutations must occur in both copies of tumor 
suppressors before cancer can develop. This is akin to having both hand and foot br akes on a 
bike. If both brake systems are working, you can stop without a problem. If your hand brake is 
no longer working, you will still be able to stop thanks to the foot brake, and vice versa. 
However, if neither brake system is operational, you won’t be able to stop. As long as one copy 
of a tumor suppressor gene is functional, the cell can halt replication and repair damage or 
destroy itself if needed. Once both copies of the tumor suppressor gene are lost, the cell can no 
longer stop replication or r epair mutations, leading to an increased opportunity for cancer to 
occur.  
 Random mutations accumulate over time. The typo is copied repeatedly,  and new ones 
continue to occur by chance. For this reason, spontaneous cancer (meaning no existing 
predisposition to it) is more common at older ages . The random mutations accumulate over the 
entire lifetime and are more likely to result in a mutation com bination that promotes cancer.  
  
Genetic Predispositions  
 You inherit your DNA from your parents . You receive one copy of every gene from your 
mother in the egg, and one copy of every gene from your father in the sperm. When the two cells 
combine, it forms a unique individual with a mixture of DNA from both parents!  
If your parents possess a mutation  in their germline cells (egg and sperm), it is possible 
for you to inherit that mutation. You then have an increased risk of developing cancer from birth ,

10 
 called a genetic predisposition . For example, if the mutation is in a tumor suppressor, you have 
already lost the function of one copy of that gene . Thus, you only need to have a random 
mutation in the on ly other working copy to potentially develop cancer. In comparison, an 
individual without the inherited mutation would need to experience two random muta tions in 
order to remove both working copies.  
 There are certain inherited mutations that are known to be associated with cancer. 
Perhaps the best known one is BRCA (which will be discussed in “What causes Breast 
Cancer?”). Inherited mutations do not gua rantee that you will develop cancer. It simply means 
that 
your chances of developing cancer in your lifetime are  significantly increased . Because your 
DNA has already been altered in a manner that is conducive to cancer development  (a

11 
 predisposition) , there needs to be fewer random mutations before it can occur. Cancer will often 
develop at younger ages when genetically inherited mutations are involved. An early onset of 
breast cancer is considered to occur prior to 45 years of age. The chances of developing cancer 
increase with age. Therefore, an early age of onset can be an  indication whether  a genetic 
predisposition is likely present  within the family .  
 
External  Factors  
 Different factors in your environment can increase your risk of  cancer because they 
increase your chance of DNA mutations. These factors are known as “carcinogens”  (kahr -sin-uh-
juhns) or “mutagens”  (myoo -tuh-juhns).  Carcinogens can be found  in various forms.  
 They can often be present in the environment around you. For example, too much sun 
exposure can cause DNA mutations due to the UV radiation. Skin cancer is often the result of 
repeated exposure to intense sun rays. There may also be certain foods and chemi cals in your 
diet that are considered mutagens. Your job site may be in a location or use substances that are 
mutagenic. People who work in mines often have higher instances of cancer, from breathing in 
dirty air. Smoking has long been known to drastically  increase your chances of lung cancer. This 
is because there are multiple mutagenic substances that enter your lungs when you inhale the 
smoke. Over time, these mutagens will actually insert themselves into your DNA. This wreaks 
havoc on lung cells and sig nificantly increases your risk of cancer the longer you smoke.  
In modern society, it is nearly impossible to avoid all mutagens, but you want to limit 
your exposure as much as possible. Repeated exposure to known mutagens causes a drastic 
increase in your  cell’s random mutation rate. Therefore, it is best to take precaution when 
available, such as wearing sunscreen or avoiding smoking.

12 
 Hallmarks of Cancer  
Each individual case is unique, but some characteristics exist that unite all cancers together.  
1. Excessive Proliferation   
2. Survival  
3. Metastasis  
4. Immortality  
5. Angiogenesis

13 
 Excessive Proliferation  
 Cancer cells replicate rapidly and without control , called excessive proliferation  (pruh -lif-
uh-rey-shuhn) . The cells increase production of any factors that promote division. They will also 
decrease production of any proteins that would normally regulate  division. The combination  
causes a cancer cell to replicate beyond normal standards. This characteristic leads directly to a 
buildup of cells and the formation of tumors.  
 
Survival  
 Your body has mechanisms in place to deal with problems in cell division when they 
arise. It is es timated that two trillion cells divide every day in a single human. There is a chance 
that a mistake can occur every time a cell divides. The cells must go through a series of checks 
before it can undergo division. If a cell fails these checkpoints, it wil l trigger repair methods to 
fix whatever damage caused the cell to fail.  If the damage is too extensive, the cell will destroy 
itself in a process called “apoptosis”  (ap-uhp-toh-sis). This procedure is commonly referred to as 
a single cell suicide. It exists to prevent mutations from being replicated and lead to uncontrolled 
cell growth . 
 A key characteristic of cancer cells is thus their ability to survive. They must avoid 
apoptosis at all costs. Cancer cells want to replicate as quickly as possible, regardless of the 
condition of the cell. They will upregulate genes that help them bypass damage checkpoints. 
Thus, the cancer cells can avoid triggering apoptosis even if they continue to acquire damage as 
a consequence of excessive proliferation.  
 
Metastasis  
 “Metastasis” is the process  in which cancer cells spread from the primary tumor sit e and 
into other parts of the body. A tumor is considered cancerous once the cells are able to leave 
their original site and travel through the body. The cancer cells break tethering connections with 
their neighbors , giving them the ability to move . They will often change their shape to make 
movement easier. Certain cancer cells squeeze into the bloodstream and use it like a highway to 
travel throughout the body.  Occasionally, when they reach a new location,  they exit the 
bloodstream and implant the mselves in a new tissue. They can then colonize this foreign location 
and begin to grow a new, secondary tumor.  
 Once tumor cells reach a new part of the body, they may also enter a hibernation state 
known as “quiescence”  (kwahy -es-uhns) . In this state, t he cancer cells will masquerade as 
normal cells.  They will maintain their replicative abilities but will not proliferate . This can allow 
them to remain undetected. Quiescence is often the explanation for why cancers can sometimes 
reappear years late r. The cancer cells may exit their dormant state  and begin to grow again.  
 
Immortality  
 Normal cells have a limit to how many times it can replicate itself. Once the limit is 
reached, the cell will no longer divide. As discussed previously, cells occasionally experience

14 
 some DNA damage during everyday activities. Having a replication limit pr events cells with 
nonspecific damage from replicating indefinitely and potentially becoming cancerous.  
Each time a cell replicates, it loses a little bit off the end of the DNA. In order to  prevent 
the loss of important genes, DNA ends are  capped with sections called “telomeres”  (tee-luh-
meers).  The telomere sections will be shortened instead of the DNA itself. Telomeres function 
much like the plastic tips at the end of your shoelaces. They protect the rest of the shoelace from 
fraying. If the telomeres are gone, the DNA will begin to acquire damage and fall apart. Thus, 
telomeres help the cell keep track of how many times it has divided, like a personal clock. Once 
the telomeres reach a cert ain length, the cell will no longer replicate itself.  
Cancer cells bypass this replication limit by encouraging the cell to lengthen telomeres 
regardless of the cellular clock. They continue to lengthen the protection segments to keep 
replicating. Thus, t he cancer cells have essentially achieved immortality.  
 
Angiogenesis  
 Cancer cells must be able to acquire enough nutrients for them  to survive. They take 
advantage of your body’s own systems  to do this. Cancer cells secrete molecular factors that tell 
your blood vessels to grow towards the tumor.  The molecules spread out from the tumor and into 
the surrounding tissue. There are higher concentrations of these factors closer to the tumor, 
creati ng a gradient for the blood vessels to follow. Much like someone following a pleasant 
smell, new blood vessels begin to grow towards the higher concentration of these attractive 
factors. This process is called “angiogenesis”  (an-jee-oh-jen-uh-sis). The ves sels supply the 
tumor with oxygen and other nutrients. T he blood vessels also provide a potential escape route 
for the cancerous cells to invade other parts of the body.

15 
 Breast Cancer  
Breast cancer is a cancer that originates in the breast tissue. It is a hormonally driven 
cancer that most commonly  affects women. In the United States on average, approximately 1 in 
8 women will develop breast cancer in her lifetime versus approximately 1  in 833 men.  
 
 
Anatomy of the Breast

16 
 What Causes Breast Cancer?  
Risk Factors  
Multiple  factors can place a person at increased risk of developing breast cancer :  
Age – The older a person gets, the more likely they have accumulated  mutations over 
their lifetime. Breast cancer is more common in individuals over the age of 55.  
Biological Sex  – Females are at an increased risk for developing breast cancer when 
compared t o males. When misused, the hormone estrogen is known to promote breast 
cancer formation.  
Reproductive History  – Women with a history of early menstruation (starting earlier than 
age 12) or late menopause (starting after age 55), are at an increased risk o f developing 
breast cancer. These women experience more estrogen production and exposure over 
their lifetime.  
Lifestyle  – Maintaining a healthy diet and exercise regime can help decrease the risk of 
breast cancer. Being overweight increases the risk of breast cancer in both men and 
women. Fat cells can act as secondary sources of hormones  that fuel the cancer .  
Genetic Predisposition  – There are a few gene mutations that are known to increase the 
risk of breast cancer in both genders. These will be discu ssed in more detail in the 
“Family History (Genes)” section.  
 
How do Hormones Drive Breast Cancer?  
 Breast cancer is classified as a hormone driven cancer. The cancer is dependent on the 
presence of certain hormones to survive and 
grow. Breast cancer is primarily driven by 
the hormone estrogen. Estrogen  is the 
leading female sex hormone, involved in the 
development and regulation of the female 
reproductive system.  Males do not have as 
much estrogen present in their bodies, 
explaining why male breast cancer is less 
common. The hormone progesterone can 
also be involved in the development of 
breast cancer. Both hormones work 
similarly, so we will focus on estrogen .  
 Estrogen is produced by the body 
and serves a variety of functions. It is 
heavily involved in the development of 
female physical characteristics (such as 
breasts ), controlling the menstrual cycle , and 
maintaining pregnancy processes . It does 
have other functions that are critical

17 
 regardless of sex. It affects  cholesterol regulation, mood regulation, and tissue health (bone, 
brain, skin, and more). Estrogen can easily enter cells throughout the bod y by traveling through 
the bloodstream.  
 Estrogen is responsible for promoting the growth of breast tissue. T his process is not 
inherently bad. In fact, it is necessary for proper human development. However, t he presence of 
too much estrogen can trigger breast cells to divide constantly . When this occurs, there is an 
increased chance that a cell mass will build  up. A tumor may form and can then mutate to 
become cancerous.  
 The primary source of estrogen in you ng females is the ovaries. However, there are 
secondary sources of estrogen in both males and females. Fat cells and liver cells can produce 
lower levels of estrogen. They can also convert testosterone into estrogen. These secondary 
sources are often the c ulprits of breast cancer in men and post -menopausal women.  
 
Family History (Genes)  
 Ten percent  of all breast cancers are considered “hereditary,” meaning that it was caused 
partially by an inherited gene mutation.  There are certain mutated versions of genes that are 
associated with an increased risk of developing breast cancer. Inheriting these gene mutations 
does not guarantee that cancer will develop, but it significantly increases the likelihood.  
 When assessing  breast cancer risk, t here are a few signs that doctors  look for to determine 
if a hereditary mutation  is likely present . They will often ask for your family history with regards 
to cancer. This history will help answer a few questions that provide clues t o the doctor about 
whether genetic testing should be recommended. The following are some of the clues that the 
doctor will look for:  
Are there multiple blood -related family members that experienced a form of cancer? If 
so, then there may be a gene that is  being passed through the family that promotes cancer 
formation. Therefore, you may be at increased risk. Cancer is not necessarily a rare occurrence, 
unfortunately. However multiple cases of the same type of cancer in one family can suggest a 
possible gen etic connection.  
Are there any instances of early cancer onset? Breast cancers that occur in  women or men 
younger than 45 are a red flag for a genetic predisposition. Usually, cancers develop from 
random mutations. They need many years to accumulate enoug h mutations in the same cell. An 
early age of onset can indicate that some of the mutations were already present to begin with , 
increasing the breast cancer risk .  
Are there any instances of males with breast cancer  in the family ? Males are at a much 
lower  risk for breast cancer than females. Typically, males produce less estrogen, making them 
less likely to develop breast cancer spontaneously. However, males are still just as susceptible to 
inherited mutations that increase their likelihood to develop brea st cancer. Therefore, instances 
of male breast cancer in a family can potentially suggest that a  genetic predisposition  is present . 
This suspicion is increased if there are other instances of breast cancer in the family, especially if 
it includes multiple instances of male breast cancer.

18 
 Are there family members that have had more than one type of cancer? When a cancer 
metastasizes,  any secondary tumors it forms are still the same type. A breast cancer that has 
traveled to the liver is still a breast cancer tumor. The breast cancer cells have simply migrated . 
Knowing that it takes a while and multiple mutations for a cancer to develo p, it is rare to have an 
individual develop multiple cancer types without a prior disposition to cancer in general. 
Looking at family history can suggest if there may be an inherited mutation in cell division genes 
that could promote any form of cancer.  
 BRCA1 and BRCA2 (BReast CAncer genes 1 and 2) are perhaps the most well -known 
breast cancer -associated genes. If you have ever been screened for genetic predispositions for 
cancer, BRCA1/2 is likely on the list. Everyone has the BRCA genes, but there are a  few mutant 
forms that are known to be associated with an increase in breast cancer risk. The normal BRCA 
genes promote a process that precisely repairs DNA when there is certain type of damage. When 
BRCA is mutated, either spontaneously or inherited, it i s unable to repair DNA properly. This 
forces the cells to rely on a backup process to repair the damage. The backup method is not as 
precise. The lower fidelity makes it much more likely to  make a mistake and  result in DNA 
mutations. Thus, the loss of BRCA 1 or BRCA2 results in a higher mutation rate in cells. These 
cells are then at an increase d risk to develop cancer. This is why  inherited forms of mutant 
BRCA1/2 can significantly increase the risk of breast cancer in both females and males.  
 There are quite a few other mutant genes associated with an inherited increased risk of 
cancer. Many of them are involved in regulating the  process of cell replication or DNA repair. 
Gene mutants that promote rapid division or the loss of repair mechanisms can likely be 
implicated as cancer -associated genes.

19 
 Subtypes  
 There are four major subtypes of breast cancer: Luminal A, Luminal B, HER2 -positive , 
and Triple -Negative. The breast cancer subtype can influence the type of treatment that is 
prescribed. Each subtype is defined by the presence of certain receptors. In biology, a “receptor” 
is a type of protein that receives signals from the environment outside the cell, then triggers a 
response from within the cell.  
 There are two major types of receptors  that are involved in the subtyping of breast 
cancer: estrogen receptors and progesterone receptors . Both are responsible for receiving their 
respective hormone signal from the rest of the body. The receptor then induces cell growth and 
division in respons e to the signal. If a breast cancer uses these hormonal receptors (HR) to 
promote cancer growth, the cancer is deemed to be hormone receptor positive (HR+) . If it lacks 
the use of these receptors, it is deemed hormone receptor negative (HR -).  
 HER2  (aka E RBB2 or HER2/neu)  is a specific mutated form of a growth factor  receptor. 
It results in a receptor that takes in too many growth signals and is always actively promoting 
cell division. This means that the cell can continuously replicate without the need fo r estrogen or 
progesterone support. It does not depend on hormones to fuel its growth. If a breast cancer 
contains this mutated receptor, the cancer is deemed to be HER2 positive (HER2+). If the breast 
cancer lacks the mutated HER2 receptor, it is deemed H ER2 negative (HER2 -).  
 Luminal A  – This breast cancer is HR+ and HER2 -. This subtype is the most common 
across all demographics. Of the four subtypes, Luminal A cancer tend to be the most slow -
growing and typically respond well to hormone therapy treatment.  
 Luminal B  – This breast cancer is HR+ and HER2+. This subtype ten ds to be slightly 
more aggressive than Luminal A. It typically responds well to both hormone therapy treatments 
as well as HER2 targeted therapies.  
 HER2 -positive  – This breast cancer is HR- and HER2+ . This subtype tends to be more 
aggressive than the pre vious two. This subtype cannot be targeted with hormonal therapy

20 
 because it lacks the presence of mutated hormonal receptors. However, it can be effectively 
targeted and treated with HER2 targeted therapies.  
 Triple -Negative  – This subtype is HR- and HER2 -. This means that the cancer is not 
reliant on the hormonal receptors or the mutated HER2. Triple -Negative breast cancer is the 
most aggressive and the most difficult to treat because it lacks any receptor targets. However, 
research is being done on other  treatment targeting methods (described later in the “Targeted 
Therapies” section)

21 
 Stages  
 The stage of a breast cancer diagnosis provides an idea of the advancement of the cancer. 
Breast cancer staging is dependent on both the size of the tumor and how far it has spread.  
 Stage 0  – This stage is also known as a ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) or lobular 
carcinoma in situ (LCIS). Stage 0 describes non -invasive breast cancers. The breast cells have 
replicated excessively within the local area where the cancer has started. The cancer cells have 
not broken out of local constraints and the tissue su rrounding the tumor is healthy.  
 Stage 1  – This stage describes the first step in invasive breast cancer. The tumor is 
smaller than 2cm , roughly the size of a peanut or smaller . The cancer is beginning to invade the 
neighboring tissue. The invasion is sti ll localized in the breast. Cancer cells may or may not have 
spread to the lymph nodes. These are part of the body’s immune system. Lymph nodes collect 
fluids, wastes, and other foreign materials present in body tissues. You can think of lymph nodes 
like t he body’s janitorial locations. They are dispersed throughout the body and are used to clean 
up any waste or messes. Cancer cells can use the lymph node system to move around the body, 
much like the bloodstream  highway . The lymph nodes under the arms are o ften the first place 
that breast cancer cells will invade.  
 Stage 2  – In this stage the cancer is continuing to grow and spread. The tumor is usually 
larger.  The size is often between 2 -5cm. In relative terms, this is similar to the size range of a 
peanut  to a lime. The cancer has often spread to the lymph nodes under the arm and potentially 
other lymph nodes in the chest region.  
 Stage 3  – This stage describes cancers that have achieved regional spread. The tumor is 
usually larger than 5cm , meaning that it is larger than a lime. Cancer cells have  extensively 
invaded  the neighboring breast tissue.  This can potentially include the chest wall behind the 
breast or the skin of the breast. The cancer has invaded multiple lymph node locations in the 
torso.  
 Stage 4  – This is considered the most advanced form of breast cancer. This stage 
describes any breast cancer that has spread to far regions of the body. The cancer may be present 
in other organs, bones, or distant lymph nodes. It is possible that it has fu lly metastasized and has 
formed a secondary cancer tumor at another location.

23 
 Screening  
 Screening is a non -invasive procedure that is used to check for the presence of breast 
cancer before there are any signs or symptoms of the disease. Screening do es not prevent breast 
cancer, but it can help catch it in the earlier stages when it is easier to treat. These exams should 
be performed routinely to look for anything unusual.  
 One of the most powerful screening method s for breast cancer in women is the annual  
mammogram. It is a low dose x -ray that examines the breast tissue. The low dose of radiation is 
not enough to do significant damage to the tissue, even if performed yearly. The mammogram 
measures the density of the  breast and looks for areas of higher density  (appearing as a brighter 
mass on the mammogram) . It is used to detect small tumor masses before they can be felt 
externally. Areas with higher density can indicate an area of cell buildup, and thus a potential 
tumor. The breast is compressed during this procedure to allow the x -rays to more effectively 
pass through the tissue. Mammograms are currently the best method to find early breast cancer in 
most women.  
If anything in your mammogram appears abnormal, the doctor may recommend getting 
an ultrasound or  breast magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) procedure. These procedures  provide 
slightly more in -depth pictures of the breast tissue. They are capable of detecting breast cancer 
tumors that the average mammogram so metimes misses. This is especially important for 
individuals at high cancer risk. Ultrasounds use soundwaves to examine breast tissue. They are 
often recommended when a breast lump can be felt but cannot be located on an X -ray. 
Ultrasounds can also be used  to guide a needle for biopsy (further laboratory testing). MRIs are 
also a great option for women who have dense breast tissue naturally , making it difficult for an 
X-ray to distinguish locations of higher cell mass . MRIs are expensive and have a greater change 
of false -positive results ( higher sensitivity can cause cancer to be  incorrectly diagnosed when it 
is not actually present), making them impractical for yearly screening of women with average 
breast cancer risk.  
If there appears to be a family hist ory of cancer, it may be recommended that you be 
genetically screened. When this occurs, t hey are looking to see if any mutant genes are present in 
your DNA that are associated with an increased risk of cancer. BRCA1 and BRCA2 are the most 
well-known breast cancer -associated genes, as discussed in the “Family History (Genes)” 
section. These genetic screens are able to detect the presence of BRCA1/2 and various  other 
mutations that could result in increased cancer risk. The results of these tests will inform the 
doctors about whether preventative treatment or more rigorous screening is advisable.  
Another screening tool for breast cancer is self -monitoring. Wome n especially should 
perform self -breast exams often. You are the person who knows the most about how your breasts 
look and feel. Therefore, a self -breast exam allows you to monitor for any lumps, swelling, 
itchiness, or soreness. All of these symptoms coul d be an early indication that something is 
wrong. There are many tutorials online about how to perform a self -breast exam or a 
doctor/gynecologist can demonstrate the proper technique in a clinical breast exam . You are your 
own best advocate  and should alw ays have the final say over your own health and treatment. If

24 
 you ever notice anything abnormal about your breasts, or another part of your body, you should 
see a medical professional , express your observations,  and request to be professionally screened.

25 
 Treatments  
 Treatment of breast cancer depends on the location, severity, and risk factors of the 
individual being treated. Breast cancers are just as unique as the cells they arise from. They must 
be evaluated on a case -by-case basis. One therapy may work for one per son while having no 
success for another. This makes it incredibly difficult to develop a “cure -all” treatment plan for 
breast cancer.  
A treatment plan can be influenced by multiple factors. The cancer stage and subtype can 
help determine what treatment ma y be most effective. The patient’s age, general health, and 
preferences will also be considered . The following section will provide an overview of some of 
the common treatments currently available for breast cancer. Most treatment plans will contain 
some c ombination of the following.  
 
 
Surgery  
 Surgery is often  one of  the first steps in cancer treatment. The tumor will be surgically 
removed to prevent further growth. They will often remove a small portion of healthy tissue 
surrounding the tumor to ensure they remove all the local cancer cells.  There are a few different 
types of surgeries that can be chosen based on the circumstances.

26 
  Breast -conserving surgery only removes the portion of the breast that contains the cancer. 
This type of surgery aims to remove the tumor while maintaining the breast structure as much as 
possible. This type of surgery is not always an option depending on the location , size, and type  of 
tumor. Lumpectomies (luhm -pek-tuh-mees) are often followed by radiation to eliminate any 
cancer cells that may have been missed or had spread to other areas of the body.  
 A mastectomy (ma-stek-tuh-mee) removes the entire breast. This surgery can be used to 
remove one cancerous breast (single mastectomy) or both breasts (double mastectomy). For 
certain women, this type of surgery may be the preferred choice ba sed on the cancer severity. 
Double mastectomies may be recommended to women at high breast cancer risk. Families with 
inherited genes like BRCA1/2 may consider removing the breast tissue early in life to help 
prevent breast cancer from forming.  
 A lymph n ode biopsy (bahy -op-see) is also likely to be performed alongside the primary 
tumor surgery or as a separate surgery. One or more lymph nodes will be removed and 
examined. They are looking to see if any cancer cells are present  within them . If cancer is 
present in the lymph nodes, the doctors know that is could have possibly spread to other 
locations of the body. If the cancer has formed tumors at other locations, then those secondary 
tumors will need to be surgically removed as well.  
 In certain cases of high cancer risk, an oophorectomy (oh-uh-fuh-rek-tuh-mee) may be 
recommended. As the main source of estrogen, t he oophorectomy will remove the ovaries 
entirely. This procedure may be used to treat estrogen -dependent breast cancers (Lumi nal A and 
Luminal B) by lowering estrogen production in pre -menopausal women. This surgery may also 
be used as a preventative measure in women at high risk to help lower the chance of developing 
breast cancer.  
The patient and doctor will work together to determine which surgical choice is best. 
There are options for breast reconstruction following surgery. If the patient is interested in these 
options, they should discuss it with their doctors prior to surgery. This can help doctors plan the 
best course of  action.  
 
Radiation  
 Radiation therapy uses high -energy x -rays to destroy cancer cells. The x -rays deliver high 
levels of radiation to the cancer tumors. The radiation causes th e DNA in the cancer cells to 
mutate excessively. As we discussed, the DNA hold s all the instructions that keeps the cell alive. 
Radiation therapy destroys the DNA to the point that the cancer cells die.  
 Radiation therapy may be recommended in a variety of cases. It can be used to lower the 
chance of cancer recurrence in the area by destroying any individual cancer cells missed by 
surgery.  
 Currently , there are two main methods for delivering radiation: e xternal beam radiation 
and brachytherapy  (bra-kee-ther-uh-pee). External beam radiation therapy is the most common 
method for breast cancer.

27 
 In external beam radiation therapy, a beam of radiation is aimed through the skin at the 
tumor and surrounding are a. The source of the radiation is a machine outside of the body. It is 
like shining an incredibly powerful flashlight at the tumor from the outside.  The procedure itself 
is painless, much like an x -ray. The doctor will design the size and shape of the beam  to best 
target the tumor while sparing as much healthy tissue as possible. Often a small tattoo will be 
administered to aid in targeting the beam properly. Unfortunately, the radiation beam will kill 
any cells in its path. It has no way of differentiat ing between healthy and cancer ous cells. This is 
why people will often experience swelling , stiffness,  and skin changes in the treated area. The 
treated area may appear like a bad sunburn. Over time, the side effects should subside.  
Brachytherapy is a method  to deliver radiation therapy in which the source of the 
radiation is actually inside the body. A small container is temporarily placed in or nearby the 
tumor location. It contains little seeds of radi oactive material  that essentially poison the tumor 
from  within. The goal of brachytherapy is to deliver radiation directly to the tumor cells and 
avoid damage to the nearby tissue. However, individuals may still experience pain or redness at 
the treatment site. These side effects should also fade over time. Brachytherapy may not be the 
best option depending on the size and stage of the tumor.  
Radiation therapy may be prescribed before or after surgery. It is called “neoadjuvant 
(nee-oh-aj-uh-vuhnt) radiation therapy” if it is given prior to surgery. This can b e used to shrink 
the size of a tumor, making it easier to remove surgically. It is called “adjuvant (aj-uh-vuhnt) 
radiation therapy” if it is given following surgery. It is more commonly given after a 
lumpectomy versus a full mastectomy. However, it may st ill be recommended if there is any 
reason to believe that cancerous cell may remain (large tumor or difficult to remove) or have 
spread (present in lymph nodes).  
 
Chemotherapy  
Chemotherapy  (kee-moh-ther-uh-pee) is the use of anti -cancer drugs. The drugs are 
specifically designed to kill cancer cells. The drugs may be delivered through an injection or by 
mouth. Much like radiation treatment, chemotherapy may be given before or after surgery. The 
drugs use the bloo dstream to travel and find cancer cells in most parts of the body.  
 Chemo drugs target cells that are dividing rapidly. Cancer cells are constantly dividing to 
survive and spread. The drugs  force the cells to stop growing and die by interfering with the c ell 
replication process. Any cells in your body that are rapidly dividing will be attacked  by the 
chemotherapy. Unfortunately, this includes a variety of normal cells in the hair follicles, 
digestive tract, and bone marrow. These cells also take damage and  are responsible for many of 
the side effects experienced during chemotherapy. Chemotherapy is often given in cycles to give 
your body a break to recover.  
Chemotherapy extracts a heavy toll on the body  in exchange for killing the cancer cells . 
Patients of ten report hair loss, nausea, and fatigue throughout treatment. Hair loss is the result of 
chemo drugs incorrectly targeting the rapidly dividing cells in the hair follicles. This causes the 
cells that produce hair to be destroyed. Nausea and other digesti ve tract issues are the result of

28 
 incorrect targeting of stomach and intestine cells. There are certain medications given alongside 
chemotherapy that help alleviate digestive tract symptoms. Chemo drugs can also incorrectly 
target cells in the bone marrow that produce blood cells. This can cause your body to have a 
lower number of certain blood cells. This can result in fatigue, frequent infections, and easy 
bruising/bleeding.  
There is a great variety of chemo drugs. The chosen treatment will depend on the  breast 
cancer subtype and what type of gene mutations are present. The doctor will determine what 
chemo drugs are likely to be most effective. A combination of multiple chemotherapy drugs may 
be prescribed to best target the cancer. The doctor may begin w ith a strong dose to aggressively 
target the cancer, and then reduce the dose as needed.  
 
Hormonal Therapy  
 If the cancer is hormone receptor positive (HR+), as in Luminal A and Luminal B 
subtypes, the cancers may be treated with hormonal therapies. The goal of this treatment type is 
to prevent the breast cancer cells from using hormones. In HR+ breast cancers, estrogen is 
responsible for telling breast cancer cells to continuously replicate. Hormonal therapies have a 
variety of methods that work to interrupt the estrogen signal, preventing the cells from 
replicating. Hormonal therapies are able to travel and tar get cancer cells throughout the body. 
This section will provide overviews of some of the most common hormonal therapies.  
 The first type of hormonal therapy are Selective Estrogen Receptor Modulator (SERM) 
treatments. These drugs act like roadblocks to stop estrogen from contacting the estrogen 
receptor. This prevents estrogen from fueling the cancer growth. This type of treatment can be 
recommended for patients of any age with HR+ breast cancer. An example of a SERM drug is 
tamoxifen.  
 The second type of  hormonal therapy are called aromatase inhibitors. In both post -
menopausal women and men, estrogen is primarily produced in fat and liver cells. Aromatase s 
are proteins that are responsible for turning a form of testosterone into estrogen in these cells. 
This hormonal treatment prevents aromatases from working, thus preventing the production of 
estrogen in these locations. This results in lower estrogen levels throughout the body. Without 
sufficient estrogen, the breast cancer is not able to fuel its growth . This type of treatment can be 
recommended for post -menopausal women and men with HR+ breast cancer. Examples of this 
type of drug are letrozole (Femara), anastrozole (Arimidex), and exemestane (Aromasin).  
 The third type of hormonal therapy are known as  Selective Estrogen Receptor Degrader 
(SERD). This type of drug connects to the estrogen receptor in the place of estrogen. Instead of 
promoting cell growth upon contact, the SERD promotes the destruction of the estrogen receptor. 
The cancer cells are unab le to be fueled by estrogen if there are no receptors to receive it. This 
type of treatment is currently only approved for post -menopausal women. An example of this 
type of drug is fulvestrant (Faslodex).  
 The final type of hormonal therapy is called Lute inizing  (loo-tee-uh-nahy -zing) 
Hormone -Releasing Hormone analogs (LHRH). This drug type essentially prevents the ovaries

29 
 from producing estrogen. This lowers estrogen levels in the body and prevents the cancer cells 
from using estrogen as fuel. This type o f therapy is recommended for pre -menopausal women. 
The ovaries are not the primary source of estrogen in post -menopausal women, making this 
treatment less effective for them. Examples of LHRH drugs are goserelin (Zoladex) and 
leuprolide (Lupron).  
 Hormona l therapies may not be effective against certain HR+ breast cancers if the cells 
have mutated another method to fuel their growth. This is known as hormone resistance. Even 
though the drugs block the estrogen path, the cancer has an alternate route to prom ote growth. 
Therefore, the hormonal therapies alone will not be enough to kill them.  
 
Targeted Therapy  
 Continued research has revealed more genes involved in promoting cancer growth. This 
has led to the development of therapies that are targeted to specific genes mutated in cancers. A 
more targeted approach allows the cancer treatment plan to be highly personalized to each 
patient  based on their DNA . These drugs can travel throughout the body like chemo drug s. Their 
individualized nature allows them to sometimes be effective even when chemo is not.  
 HER2 specific therapies have been developed to help treat HER2+ cancers. These types 
of breast cancers are traditionally more difficult to target compared to their HR+ companions. 
These drugs work to prev ent the HER2 receptor from working. Some like trastuzumab 
(Herceptin) attach to HER2 receptors and cause the m to be destroyed by the cell. This prevents 
the receptor from using growth factors to promote cell division. It also helps target any cells 
express ing HER2 to be destroyed by the immune system.  Ado-trastuzumab emtansine (Kadcyla) 
is the next generation of this drug technology. It combines the prior drug with a chemo drug. The 
trastuzumab attaches to HER2 receptors and tags the cells for destruction b y the chemo drug. 
This method provides a way to target chemotherapy more precisely to the right cells. Other 
HER2 targeted drugs have been developed that simply prevent the receptor from working at all. 
Without HER2, the cancer cells must use another fuel source. If they do not have one, the cancer 
cells will die. Some drugs of this type are lapatinib (Tykerb), neratinib (Nerlynx), and tucatinib 
(Tukysa). All of the HER2 targeted therapies have similar side effects to generic chemotherapy. 
They have drastic ally improved the survival rate of advanced, difficult -to-target HER2+ breast 
cancers.  
 HR+ targeted therapies have also been in development. All of these drugs are used to 
prevent the cell from dividing. They each target slightly different sections of th e replication 
process. CDK inhibitors prevent this protein type  from promoting cell growth. Examples of CDK 
inhibitors include Palbociclib (Ibrance), ribociclib (Kisqali), and abemaciclib (Verzenio). 
Another molecule called mTOR is needed to allow cells to  grow and divide. Everolimus 
(Afinitor) stops mTOR from working, helping to stop cancer growth. Finally, a gene called 
PIK3CA is found in 30 -40% of all breast cancers. A treatment called alpelisib (Piqray) has been 
developed that blocks the function of thi s protein, helping to kill the cancer.

30 
  Mutations in BRCA have been implicated in many breast cancers. It only makes sense 
that this gene would be targeted for treatment. As discussed in the “Family History (Genes)” 
section, mutations in BRCA1/2 force the  cells to use a backup DNA repair method that relies on 
a protein called PARP. Certain drugs have been developed that block the action of that backup 
repair pathway. These drugs are called PARP inhibitors. They cause cancer cells to build up too 
much DNA d amage by stopping the remaining DNA repair pathway. The excess DNA damage 
can be too much for the cancer cell to survive. Examples of these therapies are olaparib 
(Lynparza) and talazoparib (Talzenna).  
 The most difficult breast can cer type to treat is triple -negative. It contains no receptors 
that can be easily targeted for destruction. However, new research has shown that breast cancer 
cells also tend to have higher amounts of a protein called Trop -2 on their surface. Sacituzumab 
govitecan (Trodelvy) has been developed to attach to Trop -2 and tag it. This tag serves as a 
beacon that then tells chemo drugs to target that cell for destruction. This is an important 
breakthrough in the treatment of triple -negative breast cancer.  
 
Immuno therapy  
 The immune system is the body’s natural defense against any threats that may harm it. It 
neutralizes bacteria, viruses, and other foreign things. It is also responsible for helping clean up 
destroyed cells . So, why are cancer cells not eliminated by the immune system?  A theme of 
cancer is that it uses the body’s own systems against it. They can evade detection in a similar 
manner.  The goal behind immunotherapy (ih -myoo -noh-ther-uh-pee) is to “wake up” the body’s 
immune system, override the cancer’s  tricks, and allow the immune system to attack the cancer 
cells.  
The normal cells in your body have a protein tag on their surface that identify them as 
“self.” The immune system will not attack cells that have this tag. You can think of this as a 
name ta g that states they are part of the healthy body. Without this tag, your immune system may 
incorrectly attack your own healthy cells. Cancer cells take advantage of this system. Cancer ous 
cells will wear  this “name tag” at high levels. This t ells the immune system that the cancer cells 
are just part of the healthy body , stopping any immune attack . The cancer tricks the body ’s own 
defenses  by masquerading as healthy cells.  
Causing the immune system to rea djust itself to target cancer cells is a relatively new and 
exciting field of research. Some  drugs called atezolizumab (Tecentriq)  and pembrolizumab 
(Keytruda)  have been developed to help with this process. The se drugs blocks interactions 
between the immun e system and the cancer -version of the “self” tag. This helps the immune 
system to recognize that the cancer cells are not actually normal. It can reprogram the body’s 
immune cells to begin fighting the cancer.  
Immunotherapy drugs are still in the process  of being perfected  and may not always be 
available as an option depending on the individual cancer case. Immunotherapy removes one of 
the layers of immune system regulation. This can be dangerous as t hey run the risk of causing 
autoimmune reactions, which  occur when the immune system attacks the body’s own cells.

31 
 However, this t herapy type holds great promise for future cancer treatments  by using the body’s 
natural defenses .  
 
Combination Therapy  
 Every patient is unique,  and their treatment will reflect that. It is common for a multitude 
of the above therapy types to be combined into a comprehensive treatment plan.  Using multiple 
therapies allows the cancer to be targeted from multiple angles to provide the best chances o f 
eliminating the cancer.

32 
 Conclusion  
Breast cancer can be a frightening topic because it seems impossible to understand. This 
guide hopes to peel back the veil of scientific confusion and supply you with the knowledge to 
better understand your own diagnosis, or that of a loved one. Breast cancer is simply your own 
cells misbehaving and should be viewed as such. It can affect anyone  at any time . Thus, i t will 
benefit everyone to discuss  cancer and improve our collective understanding.  
The initial diagnosis can be extremely scary but know that there are usually many 
treatment options available. A healthcare professional will be best equipped to help design the 
correct treatment for you. You wi ll have years of scientific research supporting you in your fight 
against breast cancer.  
This guide is merely a summary of current information of breast cancer basics. It is 
important to remember  that c ancer research is always ongoing. Discoveries are being made  every 
day that can improve our understanding of cancer, leading ultimately to better treatments. The 
future of cancer treatment is likely to be incredibly personalized to each individual.  
The best method to prevent complications from breast can cer is to know your risk factors 
and catch it early. Always attend your mammogram. Self -monitor your body at home. If you 
ever feel that something isn’t right, approach your doctor for additional screening. If you have 
multiple instances of breast cancer i n your family (especially in young females or males), you 
may want to approach your doctor about genetic testing.   
 
Arm yourself with knowledge and advocate for yourself , or for your loved one.

 Abstract  
 Throughout my undergraduate career, I have taken many classes that addressed the topic 
of cancer. It is one of the modern mysteries of biology. In discussions with other molecular and 
cellular biology students, one of the most frequent questions that we ar e asked is: “Why haven’t 
they cured cancer yet?” It is a valid question, as well as one that highlights the disconnect that 
seems to exist between current biological research and the general public.  
 My grandmother had breast cancer and it was never fully explained to her what was 
happening within  her body , what the treatments would do to her and the cancer . At least, not in a 
way that she could understand. They used medical  jargon and scientific  names that were 
confusing to her. This inspired me to  write a breast cancer guide for my Honors thesis. The goal 
is to provide accurate scientific information in language that is accessible to someone with or 
without a significant background in biology .  
 “Cancer” is a terrifying word  because its true meaning is unknown to many people. 
Hopefully, this guide can provide valuable information and knowledge that can help cancer seem 
less scary and overwhelming. A greater understanding of the disease could bring comfort to 
cancer patients as  well as their families and friends.

 Basic  Biology  
 To better understand cancer, it helps to have a basic understanding of the human body 
and how it is structured . Whether you are a science -afficionado or haven’t  taken a biology class 
since high school, this section is designed to provide that knowledge.  
 
What is a Cell? 
 A cell is the most basic unit of life.  Every living thing, including you, is comprised of one 
or more cells. There is a great range in the number of cells that create an individual organism. 
The human body is incredibly complex, containing over 30 trillion cells. Baker’s yeast is 
simpler, conta ining only a single cell, but both are living beings.  
 It may help to think of the cells in your body as Legos. They come in many  shapes and 
sizes that together build a greater structure.  In the same way, your body is  an intricate mosaic  of 
different cell types that work in unison to keep you alive. Your nerve cells allow you to feel 
sensations like touch. Your blood cells circulate  to protec t you from infections and provid e 
nutrients throughout your body. Skin cells protect everything inside  from the harsh outer 
environment. Each of these cells has a unique appearance, but all are a part of what makes you 
who you are.  
A collection of similar cell types in one area is called a “tissue.” Some examples of 
tissues include: the skin, lungs, and m uscle s. Each of these tissue types respond to different 
conditions based on the pattern  of cells that compose it. Each  cell types  may look different, but 
they all have similar structures within them. They are all mini compartments that hold all the 
tools n eeded to allow your bodily processes to take place. The differences depend on how they 
use their tools.  
Another key characteristic of cells is that they have the capacity to replicate themselves. 
A cell undergoes a process called “cell division” where it creates a copy of itself. Some cells 
undergo division regularly to repair damage. Skin cells are an excel lent example of cells with 


5 
 frequent turno ver. If you scrape your elbow, your skin cells must divide to fill the space left 
behind. Thanks to cell division, your skin eventually returns to normal. Other cells have much 
lower replication rates in adult human s. For example, neurons , the cells that make up the brain,  
stop replicating at a young age. This is why brain damage is so devastating  compared to a skin 
cut or broken bone . The neurons are not able to replenish themselves following trauma. Cell 
replicatio n is a critical process. Cancer can arise from problems in this process, causing the cells 
to replicate without control . An excess production of cells fo rms a tumor . Tumors are not 
inherently cancerous, but they can be the first step .  
 
What is a Gene? 
Most of the life -sustaining work in your cells is performed by molecules called 
“proteins.” There are millions of different kinds of proteins, each with unique functions. The 
instructions for how to make these proteins are encoded in something called “DNA ” which is 
housed in the center of every cell.  
DNA includes the information for how to make everything your body needs. It contains 
smaller units called “genes” that each instruct your cell how to make one protein. You can think 
of DNA as  a recipe book.  Genes represent the  recipe s within the DNA cookboo k. Each gene  
contains the instructions to make a single pr oduct . The final product is a protein that perform s a 
job in the cell.  It is estimated that the human genome contains around 30,000 genes. That’s a 
pretty big cookbook!  
Every cell in your body contains the same DNA  and all  cells have the ability to use any 
gene. However, the fate of a cell is defined by which recipes it make s from the central cookbook. 
A blood cell may need certain proteins that a liver cell does not. They both contain the recipe, 
but a blood cell will prepare the final dish (protein) while the liver cell will not. Different levels 
of each protein within cell  types  is called difference in  “gene expression.” Expression is simply 
the scientific word for production. Cells possess extensive mechanisms to control which  genes 
are expressed , when they are expressed,  and the amount of production that occurs .  


6 
 Cancer c an occur when there are errors in the DNA . The genes produce too much, too 
little, or an incorrect version of their protein product.

 What is Cancer?  
 The term “cancer” actually refers to a collection of diseases that have two key 
characteristics: uncontrolled cell growth and invasive capabilities . Cancer occurs when 
something goes wrong in the regulation of normal cellular processes. Cancer cells  replicate too 
quickly  compared to normal cells . This causes a build -up of  cells in a single area, called a 
“tumor.” Eventually , this tumor can become cancerous if it gains the ability to invade other body 
tissues. This process of invasion is called “metastasis”  (muh -tas-tuh-sis).  
Cancers can arise in virtually any cell or tiss ue in the body. Since cancer arises from cells 
within a person’s body, that cancer is as unique as the individual. Even cancers that are in the 
same tissue can look and behave differently between individuals. This can make it difficult to 
prescribe general treatments for cancer. Modern cancer treatment is moving towards 
personalized medicine to help deal with the indiv idualistic nature of cancer.  
 Cancer is considered a disease of self. It arises in a single individual and is not contagious 
to those around you. Since it arises from your own cells, it takes advantage of the systems that 
your body already has in place. T his makes it difficult to target cancer cells for treatments as they 
masquerade as normal cells. The goal of treatment is to avoid harming the body’s healthy tissues 
while killing the cancer cells.  
   
 
What is a Tumor?  
 A “tumor” is an abnormal accumulati on of cells that occurs when cells are replicating too 
quickly. There is nowhere for the new cells to go, thus causing  a build -up of cells in a single 
area. Tumors can occur anywhere in the body  and can vary in size from a tiny growth to a large 
mass. Tumors alone are not always cancerous, but they should always be examined by a medical 
professional.  
 A “benign”  (bih-nahyn)  
tumor is a noncancerous growth. 
This type of tumor lacks invasive 
capabilit ies, meaning it cannot 
enter other body tissues.  They 
also tend to be slow growing. If 
properly removed, a benign 
tumor usually will not reappear.  
 A “malignant” (muh -lig-
nuhnt) tumor is a cancerous 
growth. This type of tumor can 
grow quickly and spread t o other 
parts of the body through a 
process called metastasis . 
Malignant tumors will likely 


8 
 require more extensive treatment. It is extremely helpful to catch them early before they have a 
chance to spread to other body tissues.  
 Any abnormal lump or grow th should always be examined by a doctor. Even benign 
tumors can occasionally become premalignant. In this state, they are still not considered 
cancerous but need to be monitored closely for any changes.  
 
 
What Causes Cancer?  
 The primary cause of cancer is the mutation or misregulation of genes. A “mutation” is 
when any change is made to the original sequence of the DNA. The DNA in a cell functions like 
a cookbook with genes being the recipes for various proteins. Genes contain  a series of different 
bases in a string. “Bases” are the building blocks of DNA. The specific order of these bases can 
be translated by the cell into a series of amino acids, the building blocks of proteins. Gene 
mutations can be deletions (removing an or iginal portion), insertions (adding something new), or 
substitutions (changing the base order ). It is just like removing, adding, or substituting 
ingredients in a recipe. Alterations to genes can have a variety of effects on the cell.  
The mutations that c ause cancer often result in the promotion of cell division and the 
suppression of anti -growth signals. It takes multiple mutations in the same cell to develop cancer. 
Many different factors can cause mutations and will be discussed in the following section s.  
 
Random Mutations  
 Every time a cell divides, it splits itself in two. To ensure that both cells are fully 
functional, the original cell must first make a copy of everything inside it. Even the DNA must 
be copied prior to division. During DNA replication, the cell works to c opy three billion bases 
perfectly in sequence. This would be the equivalent of copying down the world’s longest book 
three hundred times by hand. There are bound to be mistakes when copying something this large. 
The cell has mechanisms in place to help pre vent and fix mistakes when they happen. This 
makes mutations extremely rare in the grand scheme of things, especially mutations that have a 
pronounced effect. However, even a good proofreader misses a single typo here and there. Typos 
in DNA are “mutations .” The mutations are then passed along through every subsequent cell 
division.  
 Random mutations have a variety of effects. Most of the time  a single mutation is small 
and will not have a significant effect on the gene and its protein product. However, in  rare cases 
mutations do have an effect. The se effects can have various impacts. The mutations could 
increase or decrease the production of  the affected protein . They could ruin the genetic 
instructions to such an extent that the protein is nonfunctional. On rarer occasions, mutations can 
give proteins new functions entirely. Any type of mutation can potentially promote cancer. 
Cancer results from the misregulation of the cell  replication process. Thus, any mutation that 
affects the process directly or indirectly could lead to cancer. Checkpoints exist to attempt to 

9 
 detect DNA damage and correct it . Thus, 
preventing the damage from continuing 
through cell generations. These 
checkpoints  are especially susceptible to 
cancer -causing mutations.  
 There are two main types of genes 
that lead to cancer when mutated : 
oncogenes and tumor suppressors. 
“Oncogenes ” (ong-kuh-jeens) are genes 
that promote growth in the normal cell. 
These gene s are often upregulated in cancer 
to promote rapid cell division. Mutations 
that cause oncogenes to become overactive 
are often cancer -causing. The other type of 
genes are called  “tumor suppressor ” genes. 
These genes, as the name implies, normally 
function  to repress growth and prevent the cell from replicating when DNA damage has 
occurred. Tumor suppressor genes must be deactivated in order for cancer to occur.  
 Your body has two copies of every gene. Oncogenes must only be mutated in one copy to 
promote cancer. Tumor suppressors require that both copies be inactivated to promote cancer. 
This is known as the “two -hit hypothesis. ” Mutations must occur in both copies of tumor 
suppressors before cancer can develop. This is akin to having both hand and foot br akes on a 
bike. If both brake systems are working, you can stop without a problem. If your hand brake is 
no longer working, you will still be able to stop thanks to the foot brake, and vice versa. 
However, if neither brake system is operational, you won’t be able to stop. As long as one copy 
of a tumor suppressor gene is functional, the cell can halt replication and repair damage or 
destroy itself if needed. Once both copies of the tumor suppressor gene are lost, the cell can no 
longer stop replication or r epair mutations, leading to an increased opportunity for cancer to 
occur.  
 Random mutations accumulate over time. The typo is copied repeatedly,  and new ones 
continue to occur by chance. For this reason, spontaneous cancer (meaning no existing 
predisposition to it) is more common at older ages . The random mutations accumulate over the 
entire lifetime and are more likely to result in a mutation com bination that promotes cancer.  
  
Genetic Predispositions  
 You inherit your DNA from your parents . You receive one copy of every gene from your 
mother in the egg, and one copy of every gene from your father in the sperm. When the two cells 
combine, it forms a unique individual with a mixture of DNA from both parents!  
If your parents possess a mutation  in their germline cells (egg and sperm), it is possible 
for you to inherit that mutation. You then have an increased risk of developing cancer from birth , 


10 
 called a genetic predisposition . For example, if the mutation is in a tumor suppressor, you have 
already lost the function of one copy of that gene . Thus, you only need to have a random 
mutation in the on ly other working copy to potentially develop cancer. In comparison, an 
individual without the inherited mutation would need to experience two random muta tions in 
order to remove both working copies.  
 There are certain inherited mutations that are known to be associated with cancer. 
Perhaps the best known one is BRCA (which will be discussed in “What causes Breast 
Cancer?”). Inherited mutations do not gua rantee that you will develop cancer. It simply means 
that 
your chances of developing cancer in your lifetime are  significantly increased . Because your 
DNA has already been altered in a manner that is conducive to cancer development  (a 


11 
 predisposition) , there needs to be fewer random mutations before it can occur. Cancer will often 
develop at younger ages when genetically inherited mutations are involved. An early onset of 
breast cancer is considered to occur prior to 45 years of age. The chances of developing cancer 
increase with age. Therefore, an early age of onset can be an  indication whether  a genetic 
predisposition is likely present  within the family .  
 
External  Factors  
 Different factors in your environment can increase your risk of  cancer because they 
increase your chance of DNA mutations. These factors are known as “carcinogens”  (kahr -sin-uh-
juhns) or “mutagens”  (myoo -tuh-juhns).  Carcinogens can be found  in various forms.  
 They can often be present in the environment around you. For example, too much sun 
exposure can cause DNA mutations due to the UV radiation. Skin cancer is often the result of 
repeated exposure to intense sun rays. There may also be certain foods and chemi cals in your 
diet that are considered mutagens. Your job site may be in a location or use substances that are 
mutagenic. People who work in mines often have higher instances of cancer, from breathing in 
dirty air. Smoking has long been known to drastically  increase your chances of lung cancer. This 
is because there are multiple mutagenic substances that enter your lungs when you inhale the 
smoke. Over time, these mutagens will actually insert themselves into your DNA. This wreaks 
havoc on lung cells and sig nificantly increases your risk of cancer the longer you smoke.  
In modern society, it is nearly impossible to avoid all mutagens, but you want to limit 
your exposure as much as possible. Repeated exposure to known mutagens causes a drastic 
increase in your  cell’s random mutation rate. Therefore, it is best to take precaution when 
available, such as wearing sunscreen or avoiding smoking.
